title: Storage Calculator Guide
summary: Understand how camera count, bitrate, recording mode, motion activity, retention, and overhead reserve turn stream assumptions into storage demand.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool converts per-camera bitrate into daily and retained storage requirements for a camera set.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it after establishing a bitrate estimate and before validating retention policy or RAID/storage protection choices.
bullets: Early video-storage design planning.
Expansion review or recorder replacement.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting.
Explaining storage, retention, and quality tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical video-storage planning terms.

items.label: Camera count
items.description: The number of cameras included in the storage estimate.
items.whyItMatters: Camera count multiplies the per-stream bitrate into total fleet ingest.
items.commonMistake: Testing one camera and forgetting how quickly the total grows at full deployment scale.

items.label: Bitrate per camera (Mbps)
items.description: The average stream bitrate assumed for each camera.
items.whyItMatters: Bitrate is the core input that drives daily storage and retained capacity.
items.commonMistake: Using the camera datasheet maximum, a lab value, or a guess without matching real scene conditions.

links.label: Estimate bitrate first
links.note: Use this when bitrate has not been established yet.

items.label: Recording mode
items.description: Whether recording is continuous or motion/activity based.
items.whyItMatters: Recording mode determines how much of each day is actually stored.
items.commonMistake: Selecting motion mode without using a realistic activity percentage for the scene.

items.label: Motion activity (%)
items.description: The expected percentage of time recorded when motion/activity recording is used.
items.whyItMatters: Motion activity controls the duty cycle of stored video in motion-based designs.
items.commonMistake: Using a quiet-period motion percentage for a busy or after-hours-critical scene.

items.label: Retention (days)
items.description: The number of days the system is expected to keep footage.
items.whyItMatters: Retention duration multiplies daily storage into total required capacity.
items.commonMistake: Treating retention as a preference without checking whether storage and failure margin can support it.

links.label: Validate retention policy
links.note: Use this to test whether the retention promise remains practical.

items.label: Overhead reserve (%)
items.description: Additional storage reserved for filesystem behavior, VMS overhead, indexing, variation, and planning margin.
items.whyItMatters: Reserve prevents the design from being built exactly on the mathematical edge.
items.commonMistake: Using raw capacity with no reserve and then being surprised when usable retention falls short.

links.label: Check RAID usable capacity
links.note: Use this when redundancy and usable capacity matter.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means storage demand is manageable for the entered stream and retention assumptions. Watch means storage margin or assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means camera count, bitrate, retention, or overhead may require more capacity or a policy change.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce demand, adjust policy, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most video-storage planning problems come from treating bitrate, storage, retention, RAID, and failure behavior as separate decisions even though they shape the same final retention promise.
bullets: Sizing storage from one average bitrate without checking peak, motion, codec, and retention behavior.
Treating nominal capacity as usable capacity after RAID, filesystem, spare, and operational reserves.
Ignoring growth, camera additions, quality changes, and retention policy changes after day one.
Failing to document assumptions before the recorder, storage tier, or archive policy is selected.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, VMS platform limits, camera-specific behavior, storage vendor guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Camera bitrate can vary significantly with scene motion, lighting, compression settings, and codec behavior.
Usable storage should account for redundancy, reserved space, filesystem overhead, and operational margin.
Retention targets should be validated against stakeholder expectations, evidence needs, and system operation.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal design review, manufacturer validation, field testing, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual VMS, recorder, camera model, codec profile, storage platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for retention, archive access, failure behavior, and acceptable image quality.
Document assumptions before implementation so future changes can be evaluated against the original design intent.
